I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. var_functions.query_db:16.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:16.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. var_functions.query_db:16.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:16.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:16.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json
import re

# Get businesses with WiFi
businesses_wifi = locals()["var_functions.query_db:30"]

# Extract state from description and create business mapping
business_data = []
state_counts = {}

for b in businesses_wifi:
    description = b['description']
    state = None
    
    # Look for pattern: "city, ST, ..." or "city, ST ..."
    match = re.search(r',\s*([A-Z]{2})\b', description)
    if match:
        state = match.group(1)
        state_counts[state] = state_counts.get(state, 0) + 1
    
    business_data.append({
        'business_id': b['business_id'],
        'business_ref': b['business_id'].replace('businessid_', 'businessref_'),
        'state': state,
        'name': b['name']
    })

print('States found:', state_counts)
print('Business data:', business_data)
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'state_counts': state_counts, 'business_data': business_data}))

import json
import re

# Get businesses with WiFi
businesses_wifi = locals()["var_functions.query_db:30"]

# Extract state from description and create business mapping
business_data = []
state_counts = {}

for b in businesses_wifi:
    description = b['description']
    state = None
    
    # Look for pattern: "city, ST, ..." or "city, ST ..."
    match = re.search(r',\s*([A-Z]{2})\b', description)
    if match:
        state = match.group(1)
        state_counts[state] = state_counts.get(state, 0) + 1
    
    business_data.append({
        'business_id': b['business_id'],
        'business_ref': b['business_id'].replace('businessid_', 'businessref_'),
        'state': state,
        'name': b['name']
    })

print('States found:', state_counts)
print('Business data:', business_data)
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'state_counts': state_counts, 'business_data': business_data}))

var_functions.query_db:16.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import re

business_data = []
state_counts = {}

    description = b['description']
    state = None
    
    # Split by comma and look for 2-letter state codes
    parts = description.split(',')
    for part in parts:
        stripped = part.strip()
        # Check if this is a 2-character uppercase state code
        if len(stripped) == 2 and stripped.isupper():
            state = stripped
            state_counts[state] = state_counts.get(state, 0) + 1
            break
    
    business_data.append({
        'business_id': b['business_id'],
        'business_ref': b['business_id'].replace('businessid_', 'businessref_'),
        'state': state,
        'name': b['name']
    })

print('States found:', state_counts)
print('Business data:', business_data)
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'state_counts': state_counts, 'business_data': business_data}))

import json
import re

# Get businesses with WiFi
businesses_wifi = locals()["var_functions.query_db:30"]

# Extract state from description by more robust method
business_data = []
state_counts = {}

for b in businesses_wifi:
    description = b['description']
    state = None
    
    # Split by comma and look for 2-letter state codes
    parts = description.split(',')
    for part in parts:
        stripped = part.strip()
        # Check if this is a 2-character uppercase state code
        if len(stripped) == 2 and stripped.isupper():
            state = stripped
            state_counts[state] = state_counts.get(state, 0) + 1
            break
    
    business_data.append({
        'business_id': b['business_id'],
        'business_ref': b['business_id'].replace('businessid_', 'businessref_'),
        'state': state,
        'name': b['name']
    })

print('States found:', state_counts)
print('Business data:', business_data)
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'state_counts': state_counts, 'business_data': business_data}))

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get businesses with WiFi from earlier query
businesses_wifi = locals()["var_functions.query_db:30"]

# Extract state information
business_data = []
state_counts = {}

for b in businesses_wifi:
    description = b['description']
    state = None
    
    # Split by comma and look for 2-letter state codes
    parts = description.split(',')
    for part in parts:
        stripped = part.strip()
        if len(stripped) == 2 and stripped.isupper():
            state = stripped
            state_counts[state] = state_counts.get(state, 0) + 1
            break
    
    business_data.append({
        'business_id': b['business_id'],
        'business_ref': b['business_id'].replace('businessid_', 'businessref_'),
        'state': state,
        'name': b['name']
    })

# Find state with most WiFi businesses
if state_counts:
    max_state = max(state_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    state_with_most_wifi = max_state[0]
    max_count = max_state[1]
else:
    state_with_most_wifi = None
    max_count = 0

print('State with most WiFi businesses:', state_with_most_wifi, 'with', max_count, 'businesses')
print('State counts:', state_counts)
print('Business data:', business_data)

# Get ratings for businesses in that state
state_business_refs = [b['business_ref'] for b in business_data if b['state'] == state_with_most_wifi]

print('Businesses in', state_with_most_wifi, ':', state_business_refs)

# Read full reviews data
import pandas as pd
reviews_file = locals()["var_functions.query_db:28"]
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    all_reviews = json.load(f)

# Filter for our state businesses and extract ratings
state_ratings = []
for review in all_reviews:
    if review['business_ref'] in state_business_refs:
        state_ratings.append(int(review['rating']))

# Calculate average
avg_rating = sum(state_ratings) / len(state_ratings) if state_ratings else 0

result = {
    'state': state_with_most_wifi,
    'wifi_business_count': max_count,
    'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
    'total_reviews': len(state_ratings)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:16.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Get businesses with WiFi from earlier query
businesses_wifi = locals()["var_functions.query_db:30"]

# Extract state information
business_data = []
state_counts = {}

for b in businesses_wifi:
    description = b['description']
    state = None
    
    # Split by comma and look for 2-letter state codes
    parts = description.split(',')
    for part in parts:
        stripped = part.strip()
        if len(stripped) == 2 and stripped.isupper():
            state = stripped
            state_counts[state] = state_counts.get(state, 0) + 1
            break
    
    business_data.append({
        'business_id': b['business_id'],
        'business_ref': b['business_id'].replace('businessid_', 'businessref_'),
        'state': state,
        'name': b['name']
    })

# Find state with most WiFi businesses
if state_counts:
    max_state = max(state_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    state_with_most_wifi = max_state[0]
    max_count = max_state[1]
else:
    state_with_most_wifi = None
    max_count = 0

print('State with most WiFi businesses:', state_with_most_wifi, 'with', max_count, 'businesses')
print('State counts:', state_counts)
print('Business data:', business_data)

# Get ratings for businesses in that state
state_business_refs = [b['business_ref'] for b in business_data if b['state'] == state_with_most_wifi]

print('Businesses in', state_with_most_wifi, ':', state_business_refs)

# Read full reviews data
import pandas as pd
reviews_file = locals()["var_functions.query_db:28"]
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    all_reviews = json.load(f)

# Filter for our state businesses and extract ratings
state_ratings = []
for review in all_reviews:
    if review['business_ref'] in state_business_refs:
        state_ratings.append(int(review['rating']))

# Calculate average
avg_rating = sum(state_ratings) / len(state_ratings) if state_ratings else 0

result = {
    'state': state_with_most_wifi,
    'wifi_business_count': max_count,
    'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
    'total_reviews': len(state_ratings)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:16.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!
